Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Giovanna Colombetti

contemporary

Enactive affectivity, living bodies, and emotionally structured sense-making.

Giovanna Colombetti is not primarily a theologian of the soul, nor does her major work set out a doctrine of immortality, resurrection, grace, or divine agency. Her explicit field is philosophy of mind and cognitive science, especially enactive, embodied, embedded, and extended approaches to affectivity. But a coherent philosophy of mind can be extracted from her work, and it has unusually strong implications for any serious account of the soul because it treats mind as living, affective, embodied, situated, and environmentally scaffolded rather than as a private mental substance or a brain-bound computational system. Her 2014 book, The Feeling Body: Affective Science Meets the Enactive Mind, is the central text: it extends the enactive approach into affective science and argues that cognition is not merely embodied but intrinsically affective.

The central thesis of a “Colombettian” philosophy of mind can be stated this way: mind is the self-organizing, sense-making activity of living bodies in meaningful worlds, and this activity is affective at its root. Consciousness is not a detached inner spectator; it is a felt, bodily, value-laden way of being situated. Emotion is not a secondary disturbance of cognition; it is one of the clearest places where cognition, bodily regulation, action, world-disclosure, and personal meaning appear as one dynamic process. If one wants to translate this into theological language, the most compatible notion of “soul” is not the Cartesian ghost in the machine, but something closer to an Aristotelian-Thomistic “form of life”: the living, organizing, affective, world-involving principle by which an organism is a meaningful unity. That theological translation is not Colombetti’s own doctrine, but it is a plausible extrapolation from her enactive and anti-Cartesian commitments.

1. Corpus and intellectual orientation

Colombetti works at the intersection of philosophy of cognitive science, philosophy of emotion, phenomenology, affective science, and material culture studies. A 2024 interview identifies her work with 4E cognition—embodied, embedded, enactive, and extended cognition—and presents The Feeling Body as her major monograph on affectivity and emotion. The same source notes that after that book she developed the notion of “situated affectivity” and was writing a second monograph on affective relations to material objects.

Her project is both philosophical and empirically informed. In an earlier interview she describes her work as grounded in an account of mind that emphasizes embodiment and affectivity; she also describes enactivism as drawing from phenomenology, psychology, biology, and neuroscience. The point is not to replace science with phenomenology, nor to reduce experience to physiology, but to build a framework in which lived experience, organismic processes, and world-involvement mutually constrain one another.

Her most important sources and themes include Francisco Varela, Evan Thompson, autopoiesis, dynamical systems theory, phenomenology of the lived body, neurophenomenology, appraisal theory, emotion science, affective scaffolding, extended affectivity, and later work on affective material culture. The structure of The Feeling Body itself is systematic: it begins with primordial affectivity, criticizes major emotion theories, develops an enactive account of emotional episodes as self-organizing whole-organism patterns, reconceives appraisal as embodied and enactive, treats bodily feeling phenomenologically, proposes affective neuro-physio-phenomenology, and ends with intersubjective affectivity in empathy, sympathy, intimacy, and bodily encounter.

2. The basic metaphysics: life, autonomy, sense-making

The deepest layer of Colombetti’s philosophy of mind is the enactive continuity between life and mind. Enactivism, as she presents it, claims that cognition is enacted by the whole living organism in interaction with its environment. Cognition is not just “in the head,” and the organism is not a detached processor receiving information from an external world. It is a living system embedded in surroundings with which it interacts.

The radical claim is that embodied life necessarily generates cognition. Colombetti is careful that “cognition” here does not mean reflective thought, linguistic judgment, or representational belief. At the simplest level, living beings discriminate what matters to their continued existence: nutrients, dangers, affordances, and conditions of flourishing or harm. This basic discrimination is “sense-making,” and it is not representation in the classical computational sense; it is a form of meaning-generation enacted by living systems.

This is why autopoiesis is central. In “Enaction, Sense-Making and Emotion,” Colombetti interprets autopoiesis as a theory of how living systems produce and consume meaning, and she explicitly reads sense-making as a bodily cognitive-emotional form of understanding. She argues that modern emotion science often over-intellectualizes evaluation and rejects the idea that the body is itself a vehicle of meaning; her alternative is to reconceptualize evaluation as continuous with organismic sense-making.

The result is a non-Cartesian metaphysics of mind. Organism and environment are not two already-complete things connected by an inner representational interface. They are reciprocally specified through structural coupling. In the Italian version of Colombetti and Thompson’s work, the enactive approach is described as treating living beings as autonomous agents that generate and maintain their own identity and thereby produce their own cognitive domain; meaning and experience arise from ongoing reciprocal relations among brain, body, and world.

This does not mean that rocks, machines, or ecosystems are conscious in any ordinary sense. Nor does it mean that bacteria have reflective experience. The point is more precise: living beings are not indifferent to their own conditions of existence. At the base of mind is not a neutral representational mirror but a living system’s practical, bodily orientation toward what matters.

3. Primordial affectivity: the deepest affective layer of mind

The key term in Colombetti’s system is “affectivity,” but she uses it more broadly than ordinary talk of emotion. In the chapter “Primordial Affectivity,” she defines affectivity broadly as lack of indifference and as concern or interest in one’s own existence. She argues that affectivity is not a contingent psychological faculty but an essential dimension of embodied existence and, more broadly, of all living systems. In this sense, affectivity is the condition of possibility for more specific affective states such as emotions and moods.

This is the pivot of the entire philosophy. Affectivity is not added to cognition after the fact. Cognition is affective because sense-making is always already oriented by concern, salience, relevance, attraction, avoidance, threat, opportunity, and care. In a 2024 interview, Colombetti says that reasoning and evaluation occur in a mind already motivated, value-laden, and guided by personal interests and involvements. She explicitly formulates the position as: cognition is inherently affective.

This thesis gives Colombetti’s work its distinctive power for philosophy of mind. Much twentieth-century philosophy of mind treated cognition as the central phenomenon and affect as a secondary coloring. Colombetti reverses the default. The mind is not first a neutral knower that later acquires feelings. The mind is a living, caring, valuing, situated organismic activity, and explicit knowledge is a development within that more basic affective orientation.

For theological anthropology, this is immediately significant. A soul, if understood as the principle of life, concern, responsiveness, and meaningful orientation, is closer to Colombetti’s primordial affectivity than to a detachable mental substance. But again, Colombetti’s claim is philosophical-biological and phenomenological, not a doctrine of supernatural survival. It tells us what kind of thing minded life is; it does not by itself prove what happens after death.

4. Emotion: not a faculty, but a self-organizing pattern of the organism

Colombetti’s philosophy of emotion follows from this ontology. Emotional episodes are not inner mental states that cause bodily effects; they are self-organizing patterns of the whole organism. In her own summary, fear, anger, happiness, shame, and other emotions recruit neural, muscular, autonomic, expressive, and behavioral processes into integrated configurations. These patterns are variable and context-sensitive, while still constrained by the organism’s evolutionary and developmental history.

This view rejects two common simplifications. First, it rejects the idea that emotions are produced by fixed internal “affect programs” that generate universal outputs. Second, it rejects the idea that emotions are caused by a linear sequence of cognitive appraisals that first evaluate the world and then trigger bodily responses. Colombetti does not deny that there are patterns, regularities, or biological constraints; rather, she denies that emotions are best understood as modular, pre-scripted, brain-centered mechanisms.

Dynamical systems theory helps her conceptualize mind and emotion as temporally unfolding processes rather than static internal representations. In the 2024 interview, she says emotions, moods, and affective states are physical and experiential processes that unfold over time; fear, for example, involves evolving evaluative perspectives, bodily events, and emerging feelings that mutually influence one another.

The consequences are broad. Emotion is not opposed to cognition, and cognition is not opposed to emotion. Colombetti explicitly warns against reifying “cognition,” “emotion,” and “behavior” into separate faculties. These are useful analytic distinctions, but in lived organismic activity they are inseparable: bodily feelings come with evaluations, evaluations come with bodily feelings and attitudes, and understandings are motivated.

5. Appraisal: evaluation is embodied and enacted

Appraisal theory traditionally treats emotion as dependent on a cognitive-evaluative process: the subject appraises a situation as dangerous, unfair, shameful, desirable, or threatening, and then emotion follows. Colombetti’s critique is not that appraisal is unimportant; it is that appraisal has been intellectualized and disembodied. In standard accounts, appraisal is too often treated as a “brainy” process separate from the bodily aspects of emotion.

Her alternative is “enactive appraisal.” Appraisal is constituted by the whole situated organism. When one appraises something as a loss and feels sadness, the appraisal is not a neutral judgment that subsequently causes sadness. The loss is already disclosed sadly. Bodily feelings are not merely responses to appraisal; they participate in the experience of assessing something as scary, unfair, enjoyable, or significant.

This is one of the most important points for extracting a philosophy of mind. In Colombetti’s framework, meaning is not generated by a disembodied intellect and then transmitted to the body. Meaning is generated bodily. The body is not a peripheral sensor or expressive instrument; it is part of the evaluative structure of experience. The Italian version of Colombetti and Thompson’s work emphasizes that even current theories that include cognitive and bodily components often still inherit a head/body and subjective/objective split, treating the head as the site of intelligence and the body as an input-output channel.

This also explains why emotion is such a privileged site for reintegrating mind and body. Colombetti and Thompson note that embodied cognition had often treated cognition as cold and non-emotional, while emotion theory had often left embodied cognition underdeveloped; emotion is therefore a natural starting point for overcoming the mind/body split.

6. Consciousness: affective, bodily, phenomenological

Colombetti’s account of consciousness is not an isolated theory of qualia or an attempt to solve the hard problem by positing a nonphysical substance. It is a methodological and phenomenological account: consciousness must be studied as lived, bodily, affective experience in relation to physiological and environmental processes. In the 2024 interview, she rejects both idealism and reductive physicalism: enactivism, as she presents it, is continuous with science but refuses to dismiss consciousness as merely private or unworthy of study.

Her preferred methodological term is “neuro-physio-phenomenology.” This means that experience and physiological processes should both be studied carefully, and the investigation should move back and forth between them. First-person data, second-person methods such as guided phenomenological interviews, and third-person data about brain and bodily activity should mutually inform one another.

This is not incidental; it is central to her philosophy of consciousness. She argues that affective neuroscience has neglected first-person methods even though emotion experience is a central dimension of affectivity. An integrated affective neuro-physio-phenomenology, she suggests, could clarify how emotion experience, bodily feelings, and brain-bodily activity relate, and could thereby advance understanding of the relation between consciousness and physical activity.

Her earlier work on bodily self-awareness is also important here. Colombetti distinguishes foreground and background bodily feelings and relates them to pre-reflective bodily self-awareness. Both foreground and background bodily feelings can be pre-reflective, differing in degree of self-presentation or self-intimation. Consciousness, then, is not exhausted by explicit reflection; it includes tacit bodily self-awareness, affective tone, and lived orientation.

A crucial implication follows: consciousness is not primarily “inner observation.” It is an affectively toned openness to a world. Feelings are not private mental objects sealed inside the head; they are modes of bodily self-world relation. This is why Colombetti’s work belongs to the phenomenological lineage of the lived body, not merely to empirical emotion theory.

7. Brain, body, and anti-brainocentrism

Colombetti does not deny the importance of the brain. Her point is that the brain should not be isolated as the sufficient or master basis of emotional life. In work with Eder Zavala, she criticizes “affective brainocentrism,” distinguishing between the view that emotional states are entirely realized by brain processes and the view that bodily processes are merely driven by the brain as master regulator. Both views are challenged by stress physiology, which involves complex interactions among brain, endocrine system, immune system, enteric nervous system, and gut microbiota.

The same anti-brainocentric logic applies to moods. Colombetti argues that it is misleading to treat the brain as the core machinery of moods because brain activity both influences and is influenced by bodily processes. Moods are embodied and situated: their physical basis includes bodily processes, and their worldly situatedness involves cultural norms, material and interpersonal scaffolding, and incorporation of parts of the world into experience.

This position is a nonreductive naturalism. It does not say that emotion floats free of physiology, but it refuses to identify affective states with localized brain states. The emotional mind is realized across dynamic organismic processes, including neural, autonomic, endocrine, immune, musculoskeletal, microbial, interpersonal, and environmental dimensions. Colombetti’s 2024 interview explicitly stresses that enactivism’s focus on living embodiment includes “wet processes” such as endocrine and immune processes, illness and health, moods and hormones, and the microbiome.

For philosophy of mind, this is a strong rejection of both Cartesian dualism and narrow neuro-reductionism. The mind is neither an immaterial theater nor the brain alone. It is the living body-world system in self-organizing activity.

8. Language, culture, and the formation of feeling

Colombetti’s affective enactivism does not reduce emotion to biology. Culture, language, and social life matter because they shape how organismic processes self-organize. She argues that language is not required for emotional patterns to occur, but enculturation, including language, can influence how bodily and physiological processes integrate into specific emotional episodes.

Her paper “What Language Does to Feelings” distinguishes ways in which language acts on affective experience. Language can report feelings, but it can also constitute, clarify, enhance, and induce feelings; public emotion labels and descriptions can make complex feelings visible and accessible.

This matters for consciousness because affective experience is not merely raw sensation plus later linguistic interpretation. Language can alter the experiential field itself. Naming grief, anxiety, resentment, awe, devotion, shame, or nostalgia can reorganize what is felt and how it is made available to reflection, communication, and social regulation.

It also matters for theology. Religious vocabularies do not merely describe pre-existing “inner” spiritual feelings. They may help constitute, stabilize, refine, and transmit them. Words such as sin, grace, contrition, consolation, desolation, illumination, devotion, and compunction are not just labels; in a Colombettian framework, they can be tools that reshape affective self-understanding.

9. Situated and extended affectivity: the mind beyond skin and skull

Colombetti’s later work develops situated affectivity: the view that affective life is supported, shaped, and sometimes partly constituted by material, interpersonal, cultural, and environmental structures. In “Scaffoldings of the Affective Mind,” Colombetti and Joel Krueger argue that human affectivity is environmentally supported by material culture, other people, and their interplay.

Her 2020 paper “Emoting the Situated Mind” proposes a taxonomy of material affective scaffolds: objects used to support, shape, and regulate affective states. She distinguishes representational scaffolds—iconic, indexical, and symbolic—from nonrepresentational scaffolds—chemical and sensory. Crucially, the taxonomy depends not only on object properties but on the user’s stance and context.

This framework is not superficial. It directly challenges the idea that the mind’s machinery is primarily in the head. Colombetti writes that understanding material affective scaffolds helps explain how the experiential and affective dimension of the human mind is shaped and structured by the environment.

Extended affectivity goes further. In “Extending the Extended Mind,” Colombetti and Tom Roberts argue that if material artifacts can become components of extended cognition under the right conditions, then the vehicles of emotions, moods, sentiments, temperaments, and character traits can also extend beyond skull and skin.

Her enactive version of extended affectivity is also grounded in sense-making: if cognition is inherently affective and if living systems can extend through environmental couplings, then affectivity too may extend through non-organic environmental processes.

The implications are substantial. A wedding ring, a devotional text, a room, a musical instrument, a smartphone photo archive, a pilgrimage site, a rosary, an incense-filled church, or a family dining table may not simply trigger internal emotions; such things can become parts of affective regulation, memory, identity, and world-disclosure.

10. Selfhood: sedimentation, projection, incorporation

Colombetti’s later work also contributes to a situated theory of self. In “The Tacitly Situated Self,” with Juan Diego Bogotá, she argues that the self is entwined with the environment not only through explicit narrative but through bodily sedimentation and projection. The self has a past not only because it tells stories about itself, but because it is embodied as sedimented body memory; it has a future not only by narrating plans, but by being tacitly bodily projected into future possibilities.

This is important for a philosophy of the soul. The self is not a bare metaphysical point carrying memories as possessions. It is a temporally thick, bodily, affective, world-involved being. Its past is sedimented in postures, habits, sensitivities, aversions, skills, scars, rhythms, and affective styles. Its future is already lived through bodily anticipation and concern.

Her work on incorporation deepens the point. Objects can be experienced as constitutive of the lived body, not only through body schema but also through body image and the seen body. These forms of incorporation can coexist.

This gives a Colombettian account of personhood: the person is a historically sedimented, future-oriented, affectively regulated, materially and socially scaffolded living being. The “self” is neither reducible to autobiography nor separable from bodily history.

11. Intersubjectivity and ethics

Colombetti’s philosophy of mind is not individualistic. In The Feeling Body, she devotes attention to how we feel others in empathy, sympathy, intimacy, and bodily encounter.

Her work with Steve Torrance on emotion and ethics explores the affective and ethical dimension of participatory sense-making, especially the ways in which people are affectively interconnected in interpersonal encounters.

The significance is that other minds are not first hidden inner theaters that we infer from external signs. In the enactive-phenomenological approach, interpersonal understanding is bodily, affective, interactive, and often direct. A Notre Dame review summarizes this aspect by saying Colombetti argues for a direct-realist alternative to simulation theory and theory-theory: rather than only inferring or simulating others’ emotions, we often empathize through bodily capacities of responsiveness, mirroring, mimicry, and interaction.

Ethically, this means affective life is not private ornamentation. It is part of how we are exposed to one another, moved by one another, regulated by one another, and responsible within shared worlds. The ethical subject is not a detached rational chooser later influenced by feelings; it is already affectively entangled with others.

12. Theology and the soul: what can and cannot be extracted

Colombetti’s own framework does not provide a theology of the immortal soul. It does not prove survival after death, resurrection, divine judgment, grace, sin, or sacramentality. But it does provide a powerful anti-Cartesian anthropology that is deeply relevant to theology.

The negative result is clear: the soul cannot be well understood as a detached mental substance that thinks independently of bodily life and merely uses the body as an instrument. Colombetti’s mind is not a res cogitans. It is not a brain-bound computer either. It is living, affective, embodied, situated, dynamically self-organizing, and world-involving.

The positive theological analogue is hylomorphism. Rebekah Wallace’s theological work explicitly argues that embodied cognition challenges substance-dualist Christian accounts of the soul but supports a hylomorphic account. In her summary, embodied cognition shows mind as linked with embodiment, affective states, and environment; it challenges the idea that mind is a separate substance, but she argues that hylomorphic dualism—where soul is the form of a living body—can safeguard against both physicalism and Cartesian dualism.

Wallace’s argument is useful because it names what Colombetti’s thought makes plausible without itself theologizing: the soul, if retained, should be understood as the organizing form of living embodiment, not as an extra object inserted into a body. Wallace presents the hylomorphic soul as the substantial form of the body, the principle by which a living being is the kind of living being it is, not a ghost infused into a flesh statue.

This hylomorphic translation fits Colombetti’s enactivism better than either Cartesian dualism or reductive materialism. It preserves the unity of the organism, the non-localized character of cognition, the inseparability of thought and affect, and the idea that the whole person understands by means of the soul rather than the soul alone understanding while the body merely executes. Wallace explicitly cites Aquinas through Stump to the effect that it is more appropriate to say that a human being understands by means of the soul than simply that the soul understands.

The hard theological question remains postmortem continuity. Colombetti’s framework by itself gives no doctrine of how personal identity persists between death and resurrection. Wallace argues that a hylomorphic account can address this by treating the soul as individuating form, but this is a theological-metaphysical addition, not an entailment of enactive affective science.

13. Religious practices as affective scaffolds, not distractions

The theological relevance of Colombetti’s later work becomes very concrete in her treatment of material affective scaffolds. She explicitly discusses religious practices and symbolic material objects as tools of affective transformation. Crosses, candles, incense, relics, labyrinths, precious objects, and ritual spaces are not treated as decorative extras; they are part of how religious affective life is shaped.

Her analysis of the Catholic Easter Vigil is especially important. She describes the movement from darkness to light, the Paschal candle, the congregation’s candles, and the symbolic association of light with Christ’s victory over sin and death. Her point is not theological apologetics; it is affective phenomenology and material scaffolding. The ritual uses darkness and light not merely to represent salvation through Christ’s resurrection, but to move the congregation affectively, producing a transition from gloom and isolation to uplift and togetherness.

This is a major insight for theology of liturgy. Liturgy is not merely the public expression of private beliefs. It is a material, symbolic, sensory, communal practice that helps form what worshippers can feel, attend to, remember, desire, fear, hope for, and recognize as meaningful. In a Colombettian framework, religious practice is affective world-making.

The same paper discusses sacred art. Colombetti describes how learning the symbolic meanings in a Flemish still life—cherries and strawberries as souls, a dragonfly as the devil—changed her affective response to the painting. Symbolic understanding altered perception and feeling.

She also discusses incense and entheogenic ritual substances. Incense can function as a sensory scaffold through scent and possibly also as a psychoactive scaffold; entheogens used in ritual contexts can combine psychoactive effects with symbolic meanings connected to spiritual goals, communication with a spiritual realm, or divine medicine.

This framework makes theological comments indispensable rather than distracting. Religious symbols, rituals, devotional objects, architecture, music, incense, fasting, kneeling, icons, vestments, rosaries, and pilgrimage routes are not secondary to “real” cognition. They are among the ways spiritual consciousness is formed, stabilized, and enacted.

14. Extended religious cognition and devotion

Colombetti and Roberts themselves use religious faith as an example of extended affectivity. They argue that devotional texts, material objects, and practices can support religious faith, and that manipulating a rosary to enable experiences of devotion can count as part of the vehicle of a faithful disposition or sentiment.

Joel Krueger’s work on extended religious cognition develops this point further. He argues that devotional texts, Catholic rosaries, and Hindu Japa Mala prayer beads can count as part of the physical process constituting faithful disposition or religious sentiment, drawing on Colombetti and Roberts. He also argues that rituals, music, relics, scriptures, statues, and buildings should not be treated as mere ethnographic decoration around inner cognition, but as central components of religious thought.

The theological upshot is strong: faith is not merely assent to propositions plus private feeling. Faith can be materially scaffolded, ritually sustained, bodily enacted, communally regulated, and affectively extended. That does not reduce faith to objects or rituals; it means that religious mentality is often distributed through the embodied practices by which a believer inhabits a world as sacred.

This also complicates Protestant, Catholic, Orthodox, Hindu, Buddhist, and other debates over material devotion. From a Colombettian standpoint, the question is not whether objects “replace” belief, but how objects, gestures, symbols, and practices participate in sustaining affective dispositions and religious sense-making.

15. Spiritual and contemplative traditions: caution without dismissal

The 2024 interview includes a discussion of Asian philosophies, meditation, selfhood, atman, Brahman, subtle body, Tai Chi, and Qi Gong. Colombetti explicitly says that how enactivism relates to notions such as selfhood, meta-consciousness, atman, and Brahman is complex and not something she addresses there. She also expresses interest in subtle body experience while cautioning against easy empirical claims that quantum physics corroborates subtle-body doctrines.

This is a useful methodological stance. She does not dismiss contemplative or subtle bodily experience as nonsense. But she also resists turning difficult phenomenological or spiritual claims into premature scientific assertions. The right approach, in her framework, is careful phenomenology plus careful science, without collapsing one into the other.

For theology and comparative philosophy, this is a disciplined middle path. Religious experience should be taken seriously as lived experience, affective practice, bodily cultivation, and world-disclosure. But claims about subatomic particles, metaphysical souls, divine agency, or postmortem identity require additional methods and arguments.

16. Multilingual reception and interpretive currents

The Italian reception is especially important because Colombetti and Thompson’s “Il corpo e il vissuto affettivo” is not merely an external interpretation; the Italian version was translated by Colombetti herself. It frames the study of emotion as historically marked by a sharp mind/body split, criticizes cognitivism’s disembodied account of appraisal, and argues that emotions should be a privileged starting point for reintegrating mind and body.

The same Italian text gives one of the clearest formulations of the enactive basis: living beings are autonomous agents that generate and maintain identity, produce their own cognitive domain, and enact a world of significance through organism-environment coupling. It also states that experience is not epiphenomenal but central to a proper conception of mind.

Italian secondary reception has also developed the theme of situated affectivity. Giulia Piredda’s Italian overview presents situated affectivity as a recent and prolific development at the boundary of philosophy of emotion and situated cognition, introducing affective scaffolds, affective artifacts, and extended affectivity. She specifically notes that Colombetti and Krueger introduced affective scaffolding to explain how agents manipulate their environments for affective, not only cognitive, purposes.

Piredda also notes that Colombetti and Roberts were the first to defend systematically the application of extended-mind reasoning not only to cognitive states but to affective states, including emotions, moods, sentiments, temperaments, and character traits.

Spanish-language reception tends to place Colombetti within a post-cognitivist reorientation of emotion theory. Andrea Melamed’s “Enactivismo y emoción” frames enactivism as a way to approach the emotional phenomenon without forcing it into inherited standards, and as part of a cognitive-scientific revolution that vindicates alternative conceptions of mind and life.

Another Spanish chapter presents Colombetti and Thompson as part of a modern embodied perspective that avoids Cartesian dualism by treating mind, body, and environment transactionally. It emphasizes autonomous agents, relational worlds, embodied action, the centrality of experience, and the extension of the mental beyond the skull.

A Spanish thesis on whether emotions are representations gives a useful critical reception. It recognizes that Colombetti’s enactivism explains motivational aspects of emotion, phenomenology, animal and child emotion, and the continuity between life and mind; but it argues that enactivist theories only partly explain intentionality and face difficulties with emotional normativity, rational assessment, and high-level cognition involving abstract representations.

That Spanish critique is valuable because it identifies a real pressure point. Colombetti is powerful on embodiment, affective phenomenology, and organismic sense-making. The harder question is how far enactivism can explain rationally assessable emotions—irrational fear, misplaced pride, guilt without fault, scrupulosity, ideological resentment, theological hope—without reintroducing some account of representation, judgment, or propositional content.

17. Systematic extraction: a Colombettian philosophy of mind

From the sources above, the following systematic philosophy of mind can be extracted.

First, the mind is grounded in life. Cognition begins not with representation but with autonomous living systems that maintain themselves and enact meaningful environments. Life is not an accidental substrate of mind; it is the primordial site of sense-making.

Second, sense-making is affective. To make sense is to discriminate what matters; to discriminate what matters is already to be non-indifferent. Affectivity is therefore not an optional module of the mind but its deepest orientation.

Third, the mind is embodied in the whole organism. The brain is crucial but not sufficient as an isolated basis of emotional and cognitive life. Neural, autonomic, endocrine, immune, enteric, microbial, muscular, and sensorimotor processes are parts of the living dynamics of mind.

Fourth, the mind is lived. The body is not merely a physical object but a lived structure. Consciousness includes pre-reflective bodily self-awareness, foreground and background bodily feelings, moods, action tendencies, and affective orientation.

Fifth, emotion is cognitive-affective-bodily action in process. Emotions are self-organizing patterns of the whole organism, not inner mental items, brain modules, or bodily byproducts of judgment.

Sixth, appraisal is enacted. Evaluation is not an abstract judgment that causes emotion; it is bodily, situated, and often already imbued with the relevant affective tone.

Seventh, consciousness must be studied pluralistically. First-person phenomenology, second-person elicitation, and third-person physiology should circulate, correcting and enriching one another.

Eighth, affective life is situated and scaffolded. People, objects, spaces, rituals, images, music, texts, and practices help regulate, shape, and sometimes constitute affective states and dispositions.

Ninth, some affective vehicles may be extended. Under the right conditions, emotions, moods, sentiments, temperaments, and character traits may have material vehicles extending beyond skin and skull.

Tenth, the self is sedimented and projected. Personal identity is not merely narrative memory; it is bodily history, habit, affective style, environmental entanglement, and future-directed situated projection.

Eleventh, religious consciousness is materially and affectively enacted. Devotional texts, rosaries, rituals, candles, incense, music, sacred buildings, icons, and liturgical gestures can be integral to how religious affect, faith, and spiritual self-understanding are formed.

Twelfth, the best theological analogue of this mind is hylomorphic, not Cartesian. If “soul” is used, it should mean the living form or organizing principle of an embodied, affective person, not a separate thinking substance. This is a theological reconstruction supported by compatibility with embodied cognition, not a conclusion Colombetti herself establishes.

18. Strengths of the reconstructed system

The first strength is that it overcomes the mind/body split without flattening consciousness into physiology. Colombetti gives equal seriousness to bodily processes and lived experience. Her framework is naturalistic but not reductive, phenomenological but not anti-scientific.

The second strength is that it treats affectivity as foundational rather than marginal. This gives a richer account of practical reason, ethical life, religious devotion, psychopathology, and selfhood than theories that begin with detached representation.

The third strength is ecological and cultural depth. Colombetti’s account can explain why rooms, images, objects, music, rituals, technologies, social rhythms, and devotional practices matter to mind. They do not merely decorate internal mental life; they help constitute the field in which affective consciousness takes shape.

The fourth strength is theological usefulness. It gives theology a way to take embodiment, liturgy, ritual, devotional objects, and affective formation seriously without reducing them to irrational additions to belief. It also gives Christian anthropology a strong reason to prefer hylomorphic or holistic accounts of the soul over Cartesian substance dualism.

19. Difficulties and open questions

The first difficulty is terminological inflation. A Notre Dame review notes that applying rich cognitive language—cognition, sense-making, concern, assessment—to bacteria, molds, and sponges can invite misunderstanding, even if it is inherited from the enactive tradition. This is a fair concern. Colombetti’s “affectivity” must be read carefully: primordial affectivity is not the same as reflective feeling or human emotion.

The second difficulty is empirical discrimination. The same review asks whether Colombetti’s enactive account of emotion yields predictions that clearly distinguish it from basic-emotion theory or other competing views. It also raises concerns about bias in first- and second-person phenomenological methods.

The third difficulty is rational normativity. As the Spanish critique notes, if emotions are dynamic organismic patterns, it is not immediately clear how the theory explains why some emotions are rational, irrational, appropriate, inappropriate, justified, or unjustified. This is especially important for ethics and theology, where guilt, shame, remorse, hope, despair, reverence, resentment, and contrition are often judged normatively.

The fourth difficulty is representation and higher cognition. Colombetti rejects overly intellectualized, disembodied representationalism, but high-level human life plainly involves concepts, imagination, memory, doctrine, counterfactual thought, and symbolic systems. The open task is to show how primordial sense-making, bodily affectivity, and representational practices integrate without collapsing one into the other.

The fifth difficulty is theological underdetermination. Hylomorphism may fit Colombetti’s embodied enactivism, but Colombetti’s philosophy alone does not establish the doctrines that theology often wants from a soul: postmortem persistence, resurrection identity, divine judgment, union with God, or sanctification. Those require additional metaphysical and theological arguments.

20. Final synthesis

Colombetti’s thought yields a philosophy of mind in which mind is animate, affective, embodied, situated, dynamic, and world-involving. It begins with living systems that are not indifferent to their own existence. From this primordial concern arise sense-making, valuation, action, bodily feeling, emotion, mood, selfhood, interpersonal responsiveness, and eventually linguistically and culturally articulated forms of human consciousness.

Her philosophy of consciousness is not a theory of a detached inner light; it is a theory of felt bodily world-involvement. Her philosophy of emotion is not a theory of inner episodes; it is a theory of organismic patterns unfolding through brain, body, environment, and experience. Her philosophy of self is not a theory of a mental substance; it is a theory of sedimented, projected, embodied personhood. Her later work on scaffolding and extension shows that affective life can be materially, socially, ritually, and symbolically distributed.

Theologically, Colombetti’s work is most hostile to Cartesian dualism and most hospitable to hylomorphic, liturgical, sacramental, and practice-based accounts of soul and personhood. It suggests that the soul, if that word is retained, should be understood less as an immaterial container of thoughts and more as the living form of an embodied, affective, historically situated person. It also suggests that religious objects and rituals are not distractions from “real” spiritual life; they are among the very media through which spiritual affectivity and religious consciousness are enacted.

The resulting philosophy of mind is therefore an affective-enactive anthropology: to have a mind is to be a living body for whom the world matters.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
